custom_notes: Sections 5.1 Sequences, 5.2 Infinite Series, 5.3 Divergence and Integral Tests, 5.4 Comparison Tests, 5.5 Alternating Series, 5.6 Ratio and Root Tests. Source: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 (Strang, Herman), CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Access for free at openstax.org.

question_types.label: Write a closed-form expression for the nth term of a sequence
question_types.notes: Given first few terms or a recursive definition, produce an explicit formula a_n = f(n).

questions.label: Q1-1

ordered_content.value: Find a formula for the nth term of the arithmetic sequence whose first term is a_1 = -3 such that a_{n+1} - a_n = 4 for n ≥ 1.

questions.answer: Arithmetic sequence with first term -3 and common difference 4: a_n = -3 + 4(n-1) = 4n - 7.
questions.explanation: For any arithmetic sequence a_n = a_1 + (n-1)d.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q1-2

ordered_content.value: Find a formula for the nth term of the geometric sequence whose first term is a_1 = 1 such that a_{n+1}/a_n = 10 for n ≥ 1.

questions.answer: Geometric sequence with first term 1 and common ratio 10: a_n = 10^{n-1}.
questions.explanation: For any geometric sequence a_n = a_1 · r^{n-1}.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q1-3

ordered_content.value: Find an explicit formula for the nth term of the sequence whose first several terms are {0, 3, 8, 15, 24, 35, 48, 63, 80, 99, …}. (Hint: First add one to each term.)

questions.answer: Adding 1 to each term gives {1, 4, 9, 16, 25, …} = n². So a_n = n² - 1.
questions.explanation: Look for a transformation that turns the sequence into a pattern you recognize (perfect squares here).

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

question_types.label: Determine the limit of a sequence using limit laws or L'Hôpital's rule
question_types.notes: Evaluate lim a_n by treating a_n as f(n) for a continuous f and applying L'Hôpital as needed.

questions.label: Q2-1

ordered_content.value: Find the limit of the sequence a_n = n²/2^n, using L'Hôpital's rule when appropriate.

questions.answer: As x → ∞, 2^x grows faster than x². Applying L'Hôpital twice to x²/2^x: lim 2x/(2^x ln 2) = lim 2/(2^x (ln 2)²) = 0. So lim a_n = 0.
questions.explanation: Exponential beats polynomial; formally confirm via two applications of L'Hôpital.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q2-2

ordered_content.value: Find the limit of the sequence a_n = √n / √(n+1), using L'Hôpital's rule when appropriate.

questions.explanation: Factor inside the square root; use continuity of √ to pass the limit inside.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q2-3

ordered_content.value: Find the limit of the sequence a_n = n^{1/n}. (Hint: n^{1/n} = e^{(1/n) ln n}.)

questions.answer: Use the hint: (1/n) ln n → 0 as n → ∞ (ln n grows slower than n). So n^{1/n} = e^{(ln n)/n} → e^0 = 1.
questions.explanation: Take the log, evaluate the limit of the exponent using L'Hôpital or growth-rate reasoning, then exponentiate.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

question_types.label: Evaluate a geometric series or determine it diverges
question_types.notes: Apply the geometric series test: converges iff |r|<1; sum = a/(1-r).

questions.label: Q3-1

ordered_content.value: For the sequence a_n with a_1 = -1 and a_n/a_{n+1} = -5 for n ≥ 1, write the sum Σa_n as a geometric series of the form Σ ar^n. State whether the series converges and if it does, find the value of Σa_n.

questions.answer: From a_n/a_{n+1} = -5, the common ratio is r = a_{n+1}/a_n = -1/5. First term a = -1. Series: Σ_{n=0}^∞ (-1)(-1/5)^n. Since |r| = 1/5 < 1, converges to a/(1-r) = -1/(1-(-1/5)) = -1/(6/5) = -5/6.
questions.explanation: Identify first term and common ratio, check |r|<1, apply a/(1-r).

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q3-2

ordered_content.value: For the sequence a_n with a_1 = 2 and a_n/a_{n+1} = 1/2 for n ≥ 1, write the sum Σa_n as a geometric series of the form Σ ar^n. State whether the series converges and if it does, find the value of Σa_n.

questions.answer: Common ratio r = a_{n+1}/a_n = 2. |r| = 2 ≥ 1, so the geometric series diverges.
questions.explanation: Interpret the given ratio, then check |r|<1 before summing.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q3-3

ordered_content.value: Express 1 + 1/10 + 1/100 + 1/1000 + … as a geometric series and determine whether it converges; if it does, find its sum.

questions.answer: First term a = 1, common ratio r = 1/10. Since |r| < 1, converges to 1/(1 - 1/10) = 1/(9/10) = 10/9.
questions.explanation: Recognize the decimal expansion 1.111… = 10/9.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

question_types.label: Evaluate a telescoping series via partial-sum cancellation
question_types.notes: Write a_n as a difference b_n - b_{n+1} (or similar), form S_N, cancel, take N → ∞.

questions.label: Q4-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate the telescoping series Σ_{n=1}^∞ [1/n^{13} - 1/(n+1)^{13}] or state whether the series diverges.

questions.answer: Partial sum S_N = 1/1^{13} - 1/(N+1)^{13} = 1 - 1/(N+1)^{13}. As N → ∞, S_N → 1. The series converges to 1.
questions.explanation: Telescoping: only the first and last terms survive; take the limit.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q4-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate the telescoping series Σ_{n=1}^∞ [√n - √(n+1)] or state whether the series diverges.

questions.answer: S_N = √1 - √(N+1) = 1 - √(N+1). As N → ∞, S_N → -∞. The series diverges.
questions.explanation: Telescope to a closed form for S_N, then evaluate the limit; divergent here because √(N+1) → ∞.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q4-3

ordered_content.value: Express Σ_{n=1}^∞ ln(n/(n+1)) as a telescoping sum and evaluate its Nth partial sum. Does the series converge?

questions.answer: ln(n/(n+1)) = ln n - ln(n+1). S_N = (ln 1 - ln 2) + (ln 2 - ln 3) + … + (ln N - ln(N+1)) = -ln(N+1). As N → ∞, S_N → -∞, so the series diverges.
questions.explanation: Use log properties to split into a telescoping difference; take the limit of the partial sum.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

question_types.label: Determine convergence using the p-series or integral test
question_types.notes: p-series Σ1/n^p converges iff p>1. Integral test: if f positive, decreasing, continuous with f(n)=a_n, then Σa_n and ∫f share convergence behavior.

questions.label: Q5-1

ordered_content.value: State whether the p-series Σ_{n=1}^∞ 1/√n converges.

questions.answer: Rewrite as Σ 1/n^{1/2}; p = 1/2 ≤ 1, so the p-series diverges.
questions.explanation: Apply the p-series test: p > 1 for convergence.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q5-2

ordered_content.value: State whether the p-series Σ_{n=1}^∞ 1/(n√n) converges.

questions.answer: Rewrite as Σ 1/n^{3/2}; p = 3/2 > 1, so the p-series converges.
questions.explanation: Combine the factors: n·n^{1/2} = n^{3/2}, then apply the p-series test.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q5-3

ordered_content.value: Use the integral test to determine whether Σ_{n=2}^∞ 1/(n ln n) converges.

questions.answer: Let f(x) = 1/(x ln x), positive, decreasing, continuous for x ≥ 2. ∫_2^∞ dx/(x ln x) with u = ln x, du = dx/x: ∫du/u = ln|u| = ln(ln x), evaluated from 2 to ∞ diverges. So the series diverges.
questions.explanation: Verify hypotheses of the integral test, then substitute u = ln x.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

question_types.label: Determine convergence using the comparison or limit comparison test
question_types.notes: Compare Σa_n to a known benchmark series (p-series or geometric) either directly or via the limit comparison test.

questions.label: Q6-1

ordered_content.value: Use the comparison test to determine whether Σ_{n=1}^∞ 2/(n(n+1)) converges.

questions.answer: For n ≥ 1, 2/(n(n+1)) < 2/n². Since Σ 2/n² = 2·Σ 1/n² converges (p=2 > 1), the given series converges by direct comparison.
questions.explanation: Bound the terms above by a convergent p-series.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q6-2

ordered_content.value: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether Σ_{n=1}^∞ 1/(n² - n sin n) converges.

questions.answer: Compare with b_n = 1/n². lim (a_n/b_n) = lim n²/(n² - n sin n) = lim 1/(1 - sin(n)/n) = 1 (since sin(n)/n → 0). Since Σ 1/n² converges and the limit is finite and positive, the given series converges.
questions.explanation: Pick b_n as the leading-order behavior; compute lim a_n/b_n and apply the test.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q6-3

ordered_content.value: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether Σ_{n=1}^∞ 1/n^{1+1/n} converges.

questions.answer: Compare with b_n = 1/n. lim (a_n/b_n) = lim n/n^{1+1/n} = lim 1/n^{1/n} = 1/1 = 1 (since n^{1/n} → 1). Since Σ 1/n diverges and the ratio has a finite positive limit, the given series diverges.
questions.explanation: Compare with the harmonic series; note n^{1/n} → 1.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

question_types.label: Alternating series: test for convergence and bound the truncation error
question_types.notes: Alternating series test: Σ(-1)^n b_n converges if b_n decreases to 0. Truncation error |R_N| ≤ b_{N+1}.

questions.label: Q7-1

ordered_content.value: State whether Σ_{n=1}^∞ (-1)^{n+1} / √(n+3) converges absolutely, conditionally, or not at all.

questions.answer: b_n = 1/√(n+3) is positive, decreasing, and → 0, so the alternating series converges by AST. The series of absolute values Σ 1/√(n+3) is a p-series with p = 1/2, which diverges. Therefore the series converges conditionally.
questions.explanation: Check AST for convergence, then test the absolute series for absolute vs. conditional.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q7-2

ordered_content.value: State whether Σ_{n=1}^∞ (-1)^{n+1} · n/(n+3) converges absolutely, conditionally, or not at all.

questions.answer: b_n = n/(n+3) → 1 ≠ 0, so the nth-term test for divergence applies: the series diverges.
questions.explanation: If |a_n| doesn't tend to 0, the series cannot converge by any test.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q7-3

ordered_content.value: For the alternating series Σ_{n=1}^∞ (-1)^{n+1} b_n with b_n = 1/n², use the estimate |R_N| ≤ b_{N+1} to find a value of N that guarantees that the partial sum S_N differs from the infinite sum by at most 10^{-6}. Calculate S_N for this N.

questions.answer: Need 1/(N+1)² ≤ 10^{-6}, i.e., (N+1)² ≥ 10^6, so N+1 ≥ 1000, N ≥ 999. Take N = 999. Then S_{999} = Σ_{n=1}^{999} (-1)^{n+1}/n² approximates Σ(-1)^{n+1}/n² = π²/12 ≈ 0.8224670334 to within 10^{-6}. (Explicit numerical value of S_{999} requires calculation; the bound guarantees accuracy.)
questions.explanation: Solve b_{N+1} ≤ desired error for N, then report the partial sum.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

question_types.label: Determine convergence using the ratio or root test
question_types.notes: Ratio test: L = lim |a_{n+1}/a_n|; root test: L = lim |a_n|^{1/n}. Converges if L<1, diverges if L>1, inconclusive if L=1.

questions.label: Q8-1

ordered_content.value: Use the ratio test to determine whether Σ_{n=1}^∞ a_n converges, where a_n = 1/n!. State if the ratio test is inconclusive.

questions.answer: a_{n+1}/a_n = (1/(n+1)!)/(1/n!) = 1/(n+1) → 0 < 1. By the ratio test, the series converges.
questions.explanation: Factorials simplify: (n+1)!/n! = n+1.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q8-2

ordered_content.value: Use the ratio test to determine whether Σ_{n=1}^∞ a_n converges, where a_n = n²/2^n. State if the ratio test is inconclusive.

questions.answer: a_{n+1}/a_n = ((n+1)²/2^{n+1}) · (2^n/n²) = (1/2) · ((n+1)/n)² → 1/2 < 1. By the ratio test, the series converges.
questions.explanation: Set up the ratio, simplify, take the limit.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

questions.label: Q8-3

ordered_content.value: Use the root test to determine whether Σ_{n=1}^∞ a_k converges, where a_k = ((k-1)/(2k+3))^k.

questions.answer: (a_k)^{1/k} = (k-1)/(2k+3) → 1/2 < 1 as k → ∞. By the root test, the series converges.
questions.explanation: Root test is natural when a_n has the form (…)^n.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 5 Sequences and Series, Convergence Tests (§5.1–5.6)

subject: ap_calculus_bc
source_label: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)
custom_notes: Sections 6.1 Power Series and Functions, 6.2 Properties of Power Series, 6.3 Taylor and Maclaurin Series, 6.4 Working with Taylor Series. Source: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 (Strang, Herman), CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Access for free at openstax.org.

question_types.label: Find the radius of convergence of a power series using the ratio test
question_types.notes: Apply lim|a_{n+1}x^{n+1}/(a_n x^n)| < 1 and solve for |x| < R; R is the radius of convergence.

questions.label: Q9-1

ordered_content.value: Find the radius of convergence R for the power series Σ_{n=1}^∞ (2x)^n / n.

questions.answer: Ratio: |a_{n+1}/a_n| = |2x|·n/(n+1) → |2x|. Converges when |2x|<1, i.e., |x|<1/2. Therefore R = 1/2.
questions.explanation: Compute lim|a_{n+1}/a_n|; the condition <1 gives |x|<R.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q9-2

ordered_content.value: Find the radius of convergence R for the power series Σ_{n=1}^∞ x^n / n!.

questions.answer: Ratio: |a_{n+1}/a_n| = |x|/(n+1) → 0 for all x. Since 0<1 always, the series converges for all x. Therefore R = ∞.
questions.explanation: A factorial denominator kills the ratio to 0 at any x; R = ∞.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q9-3

ordered_content.value: Find the radius of convergence R for the power series Σ_{n=1}^∞ n x^n / 2^n.

questions.answer: Ratio: |a_{n+1}/a_n| = ((n+1)/n) · |x|/2 → |x|/2. Converges when |x|/2 < 1, i.e., |x|<2. Therefore R = 2.
questions.explanation: Compute lim|a_{n+1}/a_n| = |x|/R; solve for R.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

question_types.label: Determine the interval of convergence of a power series, including endpoint behavior
question_types.notes: After finding R via ratio test, test x = c±R separately using any appropriate test (alternating, p-series, etc.).

questions.label: Q10-1

ordered_content.value: Find the interval of convergence for Σ_{n=1}^∞ x^n / n.

questions.answer: Ratio → |x|, so R = 1, tentative interval (-1, 1). At x = 1: Σ 1/n is the harmonic series, diverges. At x = -1: Σ (-1)^n/n converges by alternating series test. Interval of convergence: [-1, 1).
questions.explanation: Find R from the ratio test, then plug each endpoint into the series and test separately.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q10-2

ordered_content.value: Find the interval of convergence for Σ_{n=1}^∞ x^n / √n.

questions.answer: Ratio → |x|, so R = 1. At x = 1: Σ 1/√n is a p-series with p = 1/2, diverges. At x = -1: Σ (-1)^n/√n converges conditionally by AST. Interval: [-1, 1).
questions.explanation: Same routine: ratio for R, then test each endpoint.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q10-3

ordered_content.value: Find the interval of convergence for Σ_{n=1}^∞ (2x)^n / n.

questions.answer: From QT9, R = 1/2, tentative interval (-1/2, 1/2). At x = 1/2: Σ 1/n diverges. At x = -1/2: Σ (-1)^n/n converges by AST. Interval: [-1/2, 1/2).
questions.explanation: Re-use R from the ratio test; test x = ±R.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

question_types.label: Represent a function as a power series via geometric-series manipulation
question_types.notes: Rewrite f(x) so it matches 1/(1-u) for some u, then substitute into the geometric series Σu^n and identify the interval.

questions.label: Q11-1

ordered_content.value: Given that 1/(1-x) = Σ_{n=0}^∞ x^n on (-1, 1), find a power series for f(x) = 1/(1+x²) centered at a = 0 and identify its interval of convergence.

questions.answer: Substitute u = -x²: 1/(1-(-x²)) = Σ(-x²)^n = Σ(-1)^n x^{2n}. Interval: |-x²|<1 ↔ |x|<1, so (-1, 1).
questions.explanation: Recognize the target as 1/(1-u) with u = -x²; apply the geometric template.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q11-2

ordered_content.value: Given that 1/(1-x) = Σ_{n=0}^∞ x^n on (-1, 1), find a power series for f(x) = 1/(1-2x) centered at a = 0 and identify its interval of convergence.

questions.answer: Substitute u = 2x: Σ(2x)^n = Σ 2^n x^n. Interval: |2x|<1 ↔ |x|<1/2, so (-1/2, 1/2).
questions.explanation: Match the argument to u; the resulting series has radius 1/|c| when u = cx.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q11-3

ordered_content.value: Given that 1/(1-x) = Σ_{n=0}^∞ x^n on (-1, 1), find a power series for f(x) = x²/(1-x²) centered at a = 0 and identify its interval of convergence.

questions.answer: Write x²/(1-x²) = x² · 1/(1-x²) = x² Σ x^{2n} = Σ x^{2n+2}. Interval: |x²|<1 ↔ |x|<1, so (-1, 1).
questions.explanation: Factor out what you can, apply the geometric template to the remaining piece.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

question_types.label: Differentiate or integrate a power series term-by-term to represent another function
question_types.notes: Within the open interval of convergence, differentiate/integrate term-by-term; the new series has the same radius.

questions.label: Q12-1

ordered_content.value: Starting from f(x) = 1/(1+x) = Σ_{n=0}^∞ (-1)^n x^n (valid on (-1,1)), differentiate term-by-term to obtain a series expansion for the derivative of f.

questions.explanation: Differentiate the known series term-by-term; radius is preserved.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q12-2

ordered_content.value: Starting from 1/(1+x²) = Σ_{n=0}^∞ (-1)^n x^{2n} on (-1,1), integrate term-by-term from 0 to x to obtain a series representation for arctan(x), and state the interval on which the representation holds.

questions.answer: ∫_0^x dt/(1+t²) = arctan(x) = Σ_{n=0}^∞ (-1)^n x^{2n+1}/(2n+1). Valid at least on (-1,1); in fact the endpoint test confirms convergence at x = ±1 by AST, so the representation holds on [-1, 1].
questions.explanation: Integrate the known geometric expansion term-by-term; the constant of integration is determined by arctan(0) = 0.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q12-3

ordered_content.value: Using 1/(1-x) = Σ_{n=0}^∞ x^n, find the power series for ln(1-x) centered at 0 by integrating term-by-term, and state the radius of convergence.

questions.answer: Note d/dx [-ln(1-x)] = 1/(1-x). Integrate Σ x^n from 0 to x: Σ x^{n+1}/(n+1). And ∫_0^x dt/(1-t) = -ln(1-x). So -ln(1-x) = Σ_{n=0}^∞ x^{n+1}/(n+1) = Σ_{k=1}^∞ x^k/k, i.e., ln(1-x) = -Σ_{k=1}^∞ x^k/k. Radius R = 1.
questions.explanation: Integrate the geometric series; the constant is fixed by ln(1) = 0.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

question_types.label: Construct a Taylor polynomial of given degree for a function at a specified center
question_types.notes: Compute derivatives at a, build p_n(x) = Σ_{k=0}^n f^{(k)}(a)(x-a)^k/k!.

questions.label: Q13-1

ordered_content.value: Find the Taylor polynomial of degree 2 approximating f(x) = cos(2x) at a = π.

questions.explanation: Taylor polynomial uses f(a), f'(a), f''(a)/2; plug in.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q13-2

ordered_content.value: Find the Taylor polynomial of degree 2 approximating f(x) = ln x at a = 1.

questions.explanation: Differentiate to second order, evaluate at a = 1, assemble.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q13-3

ordered_content.value: Find the Taylor polynomial of degree 2 approximating f(x) = e^x at a = 1.

questions.explanation: Exponential: all derivatives equal at a = 1.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

question_types.label: Apply the Lagrange error bound to estimate the error of a Taylor polynomial
question_types.notes: |R_n(x)| ≤ M(x-a)^{n+1}/(n+1)! where M bounds |f^{(n+1)}| on the interval between a and x.

questions.label: Q14-1

ordered_content.value: Find the smallest value of n such that the remainder estimate |R_n| ≤ M|x-a|^{n+1}/(n+1)! guarantees |R_n| ≤ 1/1000 when f(x) = sin x is approximated by its nth Maclaurin polynomial on [-π, π] (a = 0).

questions.explanation: Bound |f^{(n+1)}|, set up the inequality, increment n until satisfied.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q14-2

ordered_content.value: Find the smallest value of n such that the remainder estimate |R_n| ≤ M|x-a|^{n+1}/(n+1)! guarantees |R_n| ≤ 1/1000 when f(x) = cos x is approximated by its nth Maclaurin polynomial on [-π/2, π/2] (a = 0).

questions.explanation: Same procedure; smaller interval gives smaller n.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q14-3

ordered_content.value: Use the Lagrange error bound to estimate the maximum error when f(x) = e^x is approximated by its Taylor polynomial of degree 3 at a = 0 on the interval [-1, 1].

questions.explanation: Identify M on the interval, apply the bound at the worst-case |x-a|.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

question_types.label: Find the Maclaurin (or Taylor) series of a common function from the definition
question_types.notes: Compute f^{(n)}(0) (or at a), identify the pattern, write Σ f^{(n)}(0) x^n / n!.

questions.label: Q15-1

ordered_content.value: Find the Taylor series of f(x) = e^x centered at a = 1.

questions.explanation: All derivatives equal e at a = 1; factor it out.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q15-2

ordered_content.value: Find the Maclaurin series for f(x) = sin x (a = 0).

questions.answer: f(0) = 0; derivatives cycle cos, -sin, -cos, sin so f^{(n)}(0) = 0, 1, 0, -1, 0, 1, … Only odd powers survive with alternating sign: Σ_{n=0}^∞ (-1)^n x^{2n+1}/(2n+1)!.
questions.explanation: Derivatives of sin cycle through four values; only odd-order derivatives contribute.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q15-3

ordered_content.value: Find the Taylor series for f(x) = cos x centered at a = 2π.

questions.answer: Derivatives of cos cycle cos, -sin, -cos, sin. At a = 2π: cos(2π)=1, -sin(2π)=0, -cos(2π)=-1, sin(2π)=0, … So only even orders contribute: Σ_{n=0}^∞ (-1)^n (x-2π)^{2n}/(2n)!.
questions.explanation: Pattern matches cos centered at 0 because 2π is a period.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

question_types.label: Find a Maclaurin series by substituting into or integrating a known series
question_types.notes: Use known Maclaurin series for e^x, sin x, cos x, 1/(1-x), ln(1+x), arctan x; substitute or integrate/differentiate to obtain new series.

questions.label: Q16-1

ordered_content.value: Find the Maclaurin series for f(x) = x e^{2x} by manipulating a known series.

questions.answer: e^u = Σ u^n/n!. Substitute u = 2x: e^{2x} = Σ (2x)^n/n! = Σ 2^n x^n/n!. Multiply by x: x e^{2x} = Σ 2^n x^{n+1}/n!. Reindex k = n+1: Σ_{k=1}^∞ 2^{k-1} x^k/(k-1)!.
questions.explanation: Sub into e^u then multiply by x term-by-term.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q16-2

ordered_content.value: Find the Maclaurin series for f(x) = sin(x²) by manipulating a known series.

questions.explanation: Substitute u = x² into the sin u series.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

questions.label: Q16-3

ordered_content.value: Find a series representation for F(x) = ∫_0^x e^{-t²} dt by integrating the Maclaurin series of e^{-t²} term-by-term.

questions.answer: e^{-t²} = Σ (-t²)^n/n! = Σ (-1)^n t^{2n}/n!. Integrate term-by-term from 0 to x: F(x) = Σ (-1)^n x^{2n+1}/(n!(2n+1)).
questions.explanation: Substitute into e^u, then integrate each term.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 6 Power Series (§6.1–6.4)

subject: ap_calculus_bc
custom_notes: Section 2.4 Arc Length of a Curve (surface area and §2.5 physical applications are outside AP Calculus BC scope and were excluded). Source: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 (Strang, Herman), CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Access for free at openstax.org.

question_types.label: Arc length of y = f(x) computed by integrating √(1 + (f'(x))²) dx
question_types.notes: Standard setup when dy/dx is easy and 1 + (dy/dx)² simplifies (often to a perfect square under the radical).

questions.label: Q17-1

ordered_content.value: Find the length of the function y = 5x from x = 0 to x = 2.

questions.explanation: For a line, the arc-length formula returns the straight-line distance — a good sanity check.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — §2.4 Arc Length

questions.label: Q17-2

ordered_content.value: Find the length of the function y = (1/3)(x² + 2)^{3/2} from x = 0 to x = 1.

questions.explanation: Choose curves where 1 + (dy/dx)² becomes a perfect square; then the integrand is elementary.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — §2.4 Arc Length

questions.label: Q17-3

ordered_content.value: Find the length of the function y = x³/3 + 1/(4x) from x = 1 to x = 3.

questions.explanation: Classic arc-length trick: the integrand is a perfect square of the form (a(x) + b(x))² when the curve is specifically designed.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — §2.4 Arc Length

question_types.label: Arc length integrated with respect to y: ∫√(1 + (dx/dy)²) dy
question_types.notes: Use when x is expressed as a function of y, or when dx/dy gives a cleaner integrand than dy/dx.

questions.label: Q18-1

ordered_content.value: Find the length of the curve x = (1/2)(e^y + e^{-y}) from y = -1 to y = 1.

questions.explanation: x = cosh(y) identity: 1 + sinh²(y) = cosh²(y) gives a clean integrand.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — §2.4 Arc Length

questions.label: Q18-2

ordered_content.value: Find the length of the curve x = y^{3/2} from y = 0 to y = 1 using integration with respect to y. (Problem 181 variant: integrate over y.)

questions.explanation: When x = g(y), integrate with respect to y; u-substitution handles the linear radicand.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — §2.4 Arc Length

questions.label: Q18-3

ordered_content.value: Find the length of the curve x = y²/4 + 1/(8x)... (integrated over y): x = y²/4 from y = 0 to y = 2.

questions.explanation: When 1 + (dx/dy)² doesn't simplify to a square, fall back on the tabulated √(a²+u²) antiderivative.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — §2.4 Arc Length

question_types.label: Set up an arc-length integral that must be approximated numerically
question_types.notes: Many real arc-length integrals are not elementary (sin x, e^x curves). The student must set up correctly and then either identify it as non-elementary or evaluate by calculator.

questions.label: Q19-1

ordered_content.value: [T] Set up the arc-length integral for y = e^x from x = 0 to x = 1. Then use technology to approximate its value.

questions.answer: dy/dx = e^x. L = ∫_0^1 √(1 + e^{2x}) dx. This is non-elementary; using numerical integration, L ≈ 2.0035.
questions.explanation: Set up the integral correctly; identify when the integrand has no elementary antiderivative and switch to a numerical method.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — §2.4 Arc Length

questions.label: Q19-2

ordered_content.value: [T] Set up the arc-length integral for y = sin x from x = 0 to x = π. Then use technology to approximate its value.

questions.answer: dy/dx = cos x. L = ∫_0^π √(1 + cos²x) dx. Non-elementary (elliptic integral). Numerical evaluation gives L ≈ 3.8202.
questions.explanation: The integrand √(1 + cos²x) cannot be integrated in elementary terms; evaluate numerically.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — §2.4 Arc Length

questions.label: Q19-3

ordered_content.value: [T] Set up the arc-length integral for y = x^{2/3} from x = 1 to x = 8. Then use technology to approximate its value.

questions.answer: dy/dx = (2/3)x^{-1/3}. (dy/dx)² = (4/9)x^{-2/3}. L = ∫_1^8 √(1 + 4/(9 x^{2/3})) dx. Closed form is possible via u = 1 + 4/(9x^{2/3}) but messy; numerical evaluation gives L ≈ 7.6343.
questions.explanation: Set up correctly; either push through the substitution or accept a numerical answer.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — §2.4 Arc Length

subject: ap_calculus_bc
source_label: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)
custom_notes: BC-scope integration techniques only. §3.2 trig integrals, §3.3 trig substitution, §3.5 numerical, §3.6 tables, and repeated/irreducible-quadratic partial fractions are outside AP Calculus BC scope and were excluded. Source: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 (Strang, Herman), CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Access for free at openstax.org.

units.label: Unit 6: Integration and Accumulation of Change

question_types.label: Evaluate an integral using integration by parts (single application)
question_types.notes: Choose u (LIATE heuristic), compute dv, du, v; apply ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du once.

questions.label: Q20-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ x sin(2x) dx using integration by parts.

questions.answer: Let u = x, du = dx; dv = sin(2x) dx, v = -cos(2x)/2. Then ∫x sin(2x) dx = -x cos(2x)/2 + (1/2)∫cos(2x) dx = -x cos(2x)/2 + (1/4) sin(2x) + C.
questions.explanation: LIATE: pick u = x (algebraic) so du simplifies and dv = sin(2x) dx integrates cleanly.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q20-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ x ln x dx using integration by parts.

questions.answer: Let u = ln x, du = dx/x; dv = x dx, v = x²/2. Then ∫x ln x dx = (x²/2) ln x - ∫(x²/2)(1/x) dx = (x²/2) ln x - (1/2)∫x dx = (x²/2) ln x - x²/4 + C.
questions.explanation: Pick u = ln x (logarithmic beats algebraic); the remaining integral is polynomial.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q20-3

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ ln(2x+1) dx using integration by parts.

questions.answer: Let u = ln(2x+1), du = 2/(2x+1) dx; dv = dx, v = x. Then ∫ln(2x+1) dx = x ln(2x+1) - ∫ 2x/(2x+1) dx. Simplify the remaining integral: 2x/(2x+1) = 1 - 1/(2x+1). So ∫ = 1·x - (1/2)ln|2x+1|. Putting it together: x ln(2x+1) - x + (1/2) ln|2x+1| + C.
questions.explanation: IBP with u = log, dv = dx is the trick for an isolated log integrand.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

question_types.label: Evaluate an integral requiring integration by parts applied more than once
question_types.notes: For integrands like x² e^x, x² sin x, e^x cos x — apply IBP repeatedly, or use tabular integration.

questions.label: Q21-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ x² e^x dx using integration by parts.

questions.answer: First pass: u = x², du = 2x dx; dv = e^x dx, v = e^x. Gives x² e^x - 2∫x e^x dx. Second pass on ∫x e^x dx: u = x, dv = e^x dx → x e^x - e^x. So the original equals x² e^x - 2(x e^x - e^x) + C = x² e^x - 2x e^x + 2 e^x + C = e^x(x² - 2x + 2) + C.
questions.explanation: Apply IBP twice (or use tabular integration with x² reducing through 2x, 2, 0).

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q21-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ e^x sin x dx using integration by parts.

questions.answer: First pass: u = sin x, dv = e^x dx → sin x · e^x - ∫e^x cos x dx. Second pass on ∫e^x cos x dx: u = cos x, dv = e^x dx → cos x · e^x + ∫e^x sin x dx. Let I = ∫e^x sin x dx. Then I = e^x sin x - (e^x cos x + I), so 2I = e^x(sin x - cos x), giving I = (e^x/2)(sin x - cos x) + C.
questions.explanation: Apply IBP twice and recognize the original integral reappears; solve algebraically for I.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q21-3

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ x³ sin x dx using integration by parts (tabular form is efficient).

questions.answer: Tabular: derivatives of x³ are 3x², 6x, 6, 0; antiderivatives of sin x are -cos x, -sin x, cos x, sin x. Pair with alternating signs: x³(-cos x) - 3x²(-sin x) + 6x(cos x) - 6(sin x) + C = -x³ cos x + 3x² sin x + 6x cos x - 6 sin x + C.
questions.explanation: Tabular integration speeds up IBP when one factor reduces to 0 after finitely many derivatives.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

question_types.label: Evaluate a rational-function integral using partial fractions with distinct linear factors
question_types.notes: Factor the denominator into distinct linear pieces, decompose, solve for constants (cover-up or equating coefficients), integrate each piece as a log. Perform long division first if improper.

questions.label: Q22-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ dx / ((x - 3)(x - 2)) using partial fractions.

questions.answer: Decompose: 1/((x-3)(x-2)) = A/(x-3) + B/(x-2). Clearing denominators: 1 = A(x-2) + B(x-3). Set x = 3: A = 1; set x = 2: B = -1. ∫[1/(x-3) - 1/(x-2)] dx = ln|x-3| - ln|x-2| + C = ln|(x-3)/(x-2)| + C.
questions.explanation: Two distinct linear factors: cover-up method gives A and B instantly.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q22-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ 3x dx / (x² + 2x - 8) using partial fractions.

questions.answer: Factor denominator: x² + 2x - 8 = (x+4)(x-2). Decompose 3x/((x+4)(x-2)) = A/(x+4) + B/(x-2). Clearing: 3x = A(x-2) + B(x+4). Set x = -4: -12 = -6A, A = 2. Set x = 2: 6 = 6B, B = 1. ∫[2/(x+4) + 1/(x-2)] dx = 2 ln|x+4| + ln|x-2| + C.
questions.explanation: Factor first, then apply cover-up for A and B.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q22-3

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ dx / (x(x-1)(x-2)) using partial fractions.

questions.explanation: Three distinct linear factors: set x equal to each root to isolate each constant.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

question_types.label: Evaluate an improper integral with one or both infinite limits, or determine it diverges
question_types.notes: Rewrite ∫_a^∞ f(x) dx = lim_{b→∞} ∫_a^b f(x) dx; evaluate the finite integral, then take the limit. Converges iff limit is finite.

questions.label: Q23-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_0^∞ 1/(4 + x²) dx, or state that the integral diverges.

questions.answer: ∫ dx/(4+x²) = (1/2) arctan(x/2) + C. So ∫_0^∞ = lim_{b→∞} (1/2)[arctan(b/2) - arctan(0)] = (1/2)(π/2 - 0) = π/4. Converges to π/4.
questions.explanation: Antiderivative is arctan; evaluate the limit as upper bound → ∞.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q23-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_1^∞ x e^{-x} dx, or state that the integral diverges.

questions.explanation: Find the antiderivative via IBP, then take the limit; polynomial-times-e^{-x} → 0.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q23-3

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_1^∞ 1/(x ln x) dx, or state that the integral diverges.

questions.answer: Let u = ln x, du = dx/x. ∫dx/(x ln x) = ∫du/u = ln|u| = ln|ln x|. So ∫_1^∞ = lim_{b→∞} [ln(ln b) - ln(ln 1)]. But ln 1 = 0, so ln(ln 1) = ln 0 = -∞, which already tells us the integral is improper at the lower limit too. Handling it properly as lim_{a→1^+} [ln(ln b) - ln(ln a)]: as a → 1^+, ln a → 0^+, so ln(ln a) → -∞. Divergent.
questions.explanation: Check endpoints carefully; even a point where the integrand is undefined inside [1, ∞) forces a limit.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

question_types.label: Evaluate an improper integral with an unbounded integrand on a finite interval, or determine it diverges
question_types.notes: When f blows up at an endpoint c in [a, b], write ∫_a^b = lim_{t→c} ∫_a^t. If the discontinuity is interior, split the integral at the problem point.

questions.label: Q24-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_2^4 dx/(x - 3)², or state that the integral diverges.

questions.answer: Integrand blows up at x = 3, which is interior to [2, 4]. Split: ∫_2^3 + ∫_3^4. For ∫_2^3: lim_{t→3^-} [-1/(x-3)]_2^t = lim [-1/(t-3) - 1] = +∞. Divergent.
questions.explanation: Check for interior discontinuities; if any improper piece diverges, the whole integral diverges.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q24-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_0^2 dx/√(4 - x²), or state that the integral diverges.

questions.answer: Integrand blows up at x = 2. ∫ dx/√(4-x²) = arcsin(x/2) + C. ∫_0^2 = lim_{t→2^-} [arcsin(t/2) - arcsin(0)] = arcsin(1) - 0 = π/2. Converges to π/2.
questions.explanation: Unbounded at the upper limit; take the one-sided limit as t → 2^-.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

questions.label: Q24-3

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_0^1 dx/√x, or state that the integral diverges.

questions.answer: Integrand unbounded at x = 0. ∫ x^{-1/2} dx = 2√x + C. ∫_0^1 = lim_{t→0^+} [2√1 - 2√t] = 2 - 0 = 2. Converges to 2.
questions.explanation: p-integral at 0: ∫_0^1 x^{-p} dx converges iff p < 1.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 3 BC-scope techniques (§3.1 IBP, §3.4 Partial Fractions, §3.7 Improper Integrals)

subject: ap_calculus_bc
source_label: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)
custom_notes: Sections 4.1 Basics of Differential Equations, 4.2 Direction Fields and Numerical Methods, 4.3 Separable Equations, 4.4 The Logistic Equation. §4.5 First-Order Linear Equations is outside AP Calculus BC scope and was excluded. Source: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 (Strang, Herman), CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Access for free at openstax.org.

question_types.label: Verify that a given function is a solution to a differential equation
question_types.notes: Substitute the candidate function into both sides of the DE and check equality.

questions.label: Q25-1

ordered_content.value: Verify that y = x³/3 is a solution of the differential equation y' = x².

questions.answer: Differentiate: y' = d/dx [x³/3] = x². Right side is x². LHS = RHS ✓. y = x³/3 is a solution.
questions.explanation: Compute y' from the proposed formula and compare with the RHS of the DE.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q25-2

ordered_content.value: Verify that y = 2e^x − x − 1 is a solution of the differential equation y' = y + x.

questions.answer: y' = 2e^x − 1. Compute y + x = (2e^x − x − 1) + x = 2e^x − 1. LHS = RHS ✓. y = 2e^x − x − 1 is a solution.
questions.explanation: Evaluate both sides separately and show they're algebraically equal.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q25-3

ordered_content.value: Verify that y = 1/(1 − x) is a solution of the differential equation y' = y².

questions.explanation: Differentiate using the chain rule and recognize the result as y².

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

question_types.label: Interpret or match a slope field to a differential equation
question_types.notes: Use the DE's sign/slope pattern at sample points to identify the matching slope field, or to sketch a solution curve through a given initial point.

questions.label: Q26-1

ordered_content.value: For the differential equation y' = -2y, describe the slope field's key features (where slopes are zero, positive, negative) and identify any equilibrium solutions.

questions.answer: Slope depends only on y. At y = 0, slope = 0 (horizontal segments along the x-axis — equilibrium). For y > 0, slope is negative (curves decrease toward y = 0). For y < 0, slope is positive (curves increase toward y = 0). The only equilibrium is y = 0, and it is stable.
questions.explanation: When f(x, y) depends only on y, slopes are constant along horizontal lines. Zeros of f(y) give equilibria; sign analysis gives stability.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q26-2

ordered_content.value: For the differential equation y' = y² − 2y, identify all equilibrium solutions and classify each as stable, unstable, or semi-stable.

questions.answer: Factor: y' = y(y − 2). Equilibria at y = 0 and y = 2. Sign analysis: y' > 0 for y < 0 (solutions increase), y' < 0 for 0 < y < 2 (decrease), y' > 0 for y > 2 (increase). So y = 0 is stable (arrows point toward it from both sides), y = 2 is unstable (arrows point away).
questions.explanation: Set y' = 0 for equilibria, test the sign of y' in each region to classify stability.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q26-3

ordered_content.value: For the differential equation y' = t sin y, describe the slope-field behavior along the equilibrium lines y = nπ (n an integer) and discuss how the direction of the slope depends on t.

questions.answer: sin y = 0 at y = nπ, so along those horizontal lines slopes are zero — these are equilibrium solutions (horizontal line solutions). Away from them, the slope sign is (sign of t)·(sign of sin y). So for fixed y with sin y > 0, slopes change sign at t = 0: negative for t < 0 and positive for t > 0 (and vice versa when sin y < 0). The slope field is symmetric under (t, y) → (-t, y) with slope sign flipped.
questions.explanation: Zero of f(t, y) locates equilibrium curves; factoring shows how slope depends on each variable independently.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

question_types.label: Approximate a solution value using Euler's method
question_types.notes: Given y' = f(x, y), y(x₀) = y₀, step size h, iterate y_{n+1} = y_n + h · f(x_n, y_n) for the requested number of steps.

questions.label: Q27-1

ordered_content.value: Use Euler's method with n = 5 steps over the interval t ∈ [0, 1] to estimate y(1), given y' = -3y, y(0) = 1. Compare to the exact solution y = e^{-3t}.

questions.explanation: Iterate y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n); tabulate; compare at the endpoint.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q27-2

ordered_content.value: Use Euler's method with n = 5 steps over the interval t ∈ [0, 1] to estimate y(1), given y' = t², y(0) = 0. Compare to the exact solution y = t³/3.

questions.explanation: Same iteration; in this problem f depends only on t so each step just samples t² at the current t.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q27-3

ordered_content.value: Use Euler's method with n = 5 steps over the interval t ∈ [0, 1] to estimate y(1), given y' = 3t − y, y(0) = 1. Compare to the exact solution y = 3t + 4e^{-t} − 3.

questions.explanation: Keep careful column-by-column arithmetic; compute t_n and y_n separately each step.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

question_types.label: Find the general solution of a separable differential equation
question_types.notes: Separate variables so one side depends only on y and the other only on x; integrate both sides; solve for y if possible.

questions.label: Q28-1

ordered_content.value: Find the general solution to the differential equation dy/dt = y cos(3t + 2).

questions.answer: Separate: dy/y = cos(3t + 2) dt. Integrate: ln|y| = (1/3) sin(3t + 2) + C. Exponentiate: |y| = e^{(1/3) sin(3t + 2) + C} = A e^{(1/3) sin(3t + 2)}. General solution: y = A e^{(1/3) sin(3t + 2)}, where A is any nonzero real (A = 0 also a solution since y ≡ 0 satisfies the DE).
questions.explanation: Isolate dy/y on one side, integrate, exponentiate. Absorb the ± and e^C into a single constant A.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q28-2

ordered_content.value: Find the general solution to the differential equation 2x dy/dx = y².

questions.answer: Separate: dy/y² = dx/(2x). Integrate: -1/y = (1/2) ln|x| + C. Solve for y: y = -1/((1/2) ln|x| + C) = -2/(ln|x| + 2C) = -2/(ln|x| + K), where K is an arbitrary constant.
questions.explanation: Move y² to one side and the x's to the other; integrate both sides; algebraically solve for y.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q28-3

ordered_content.value: Find the general solution to the differential equation dy/dt = y cos(3t + 2) ... (variant) y' = e^y e^x.

questions.answer: Separate: e^{-y} dy = e^x dx. Integrate: -e^{-y} = e^x + C. Solve: e^{-y} = -e^x - C = K - e^x (with K = -C). Taking logs requires K > e^x; then -y = ln(K - e^x), so y = -ln(K - e^x).
questions.explanation: Separate the exponentials; integrate; solve for y.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

question_types.label: Find the particular solution of a separable differential equation with an initial condition
question_types.notes: Separate variables, integrate, then apply the initial condition to pin down the constant.

questions.label: Q29-1

ordered_content.value: Solve the initial-value problem y' = e^{y - x}, y(0) = 0.

questions.answer: Rewrite: dy/dx = e^y e^{-x}. Separate: e^{-y} dy = e^{-x} dx. Integrate: -e^{-y} = -e^{-x} + C. So e^{-y} = e^{-x} - C. Apply y(0) = 0: e^0 = e^0 - C → 1 = 1 - C → C = 0. So e^{-y} = e^{-x}, i.e., y = x.
questions.explanation: Separate, integrate, apply the initial condition to solve for the constant, then solve for y.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q29-2

ordered_content.value: Solve the initial-value problem dy/dx = y³ x e^{x²}, y(0) = 1.

questions.answer: Separate: y^{-3} dy = x e^{x²} dx. Integrate LHS: -y^{-2}/2. Integrate RHS using u = x²: (1/2) e^{x²}. So -1/(2y²) = (1/2) e^{x²} + C. Apply y(0) = 1: -1/2 = 1/2 + C → C = -1. So -1/(2y²) = (1/2) e^{x²} - 1, giving 1/y² = 2 - e^{x²}, y² = 1/(2 - e^{x²}). Taking positive root consistent with y(0) = 1: y = 1/√(2 - e^{x²}). Domain: e^{x²} < 2, i.e., |x| < √(ln 2).
questions.explanation: Separate, integrate (substitute u = x² on the right), apply IC, solve for y, note the domain restriction imposed by the square root.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q29-3

ordered_content.value: Solve the initial-value problem y' = 2xy(1 + 2y), y(0) = -1.

questions.answer: Separate: dy/[y(1 + 2y)] = 2x dx. Partial fractions on the left: 1/[y(1+2y)] = 1/y - 2/(1+2y). Integrate: ln|y| - ln|1+2y| = x² + C, i.e., ln|y/(1+2y)| = x² + C, so y/(1+2y) = A e^{x²}. Apply y(0) = -1: -1/(1 + 2(-1)) = -1/(-1) = 1 = A · 1, so A = 1. Then y = (1 + 2y) e^{x²}, i.e., y(1 - 2e^{x²}) = e^{x²}, y = e^{x²}/(1 - 2e^{x²}). Check y(0): e^0/(1 - 2) = 1/(-1) = -1 ✓.
questions.explanation: Separable with partial fractions needed on the y side; solve for A using the IC; algebraically isolate y.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

question_types.label: Solve an exponential growth or decay problem from a real-world context
question_types.notes: Recognize 'rate of change proportional to amount' → dy/dt = ky → y = y₀ e^{kt}. Use two data points to find y₀ and k, then answer the question.

questions.label: Q30-1

ordered_content.value: A bacterial culture grows at a rate proportional to its current population. Initially there are 1000 bacteria and after 2 hours there are 1500. How many bacteria will there be after 6 hours?

questions.explanation: Set up dy/dt = ky; solution y = y₀ e^{kt}; use two data points to find k; evaluate at the target time.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q30-2

ordered_content.value: A radioactive substance decays at a rate proportional to its current mass. If 100 g decays to 80 g in 5 years, find the half-life.

questions.explanation: Exponential decay: find k from the given data, then solve for T where m(T) = m₀/2.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q30-3

ordered_content.value: A cake is removed from the oven at 450°F and placed in a 70°F kitchen. After 10 minutes the cake is 330°F. Assume Newton's law of cooling: the temperature difference T(t) − 70 satisfies dy/dt = k·y. How long after removing the cake will its temperature be within 5°F of room temperature (i.e., 75°F)?

questions.explanation: Newton's cooling reduces to exponential decay in the temperature difference; same y = y₀ e^{kt} formula.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

question_types.label: Interpret a logistic growth model: carrying capacity, equilibria, and point of maximum growth rate
question_types.notes: For dP/dt = kP(1 − P/K): equilibria at P = 0 (unstable) and P = K (stable, carrying capacity); fastest growth at P = K/2.

questions.label: Q31-1

ordered_content.value: A population of deer in a park has a carrying capacity of 200 and a growth rate of 2%. If the initial population is 50 deer, what is the population of deer at any time?

questions.answer: Logistic model: dP/dt = 0.02 P (1 − P/200). Standard solution P(t) = K/(1 + A e^{-rt}) with K = 200, r = 0.02. Use P(0) = 50: 50 = 200/(1 + A) → 1 + A = 4 → A = 3. So P(t) = 200/(1 + 3 e^{-0.02 t}).
questions.explanation: Plug carrying capacity K, rate r, and IC into P(t) = K/(1 + A e^{-rt}); solve for A from P(0).

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q31-2

ordered_content.value: Rabbits in a park have an initial population of 10 and grow at a rate of 4% per year. If the carrying capacity is 500, at what time does the population reach 100 rabbits?

questions.explanation: Solve the logistic equation for t at the target population.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 4 Introduction to Differential Equations (§4.1–4.4)

questions.label: Q31-3

ordered_content.value: For the logistic equation dP/dt = 0.4 P(1 − P/10000), at what population size is the population growing fastest, and what is that maximum growth rate?

questions.answer: Max rate of logistic dP/dt = r P(1 − P/K) occurs at P = K/2 = 5000. Value: dP/dt|_{P=K/2} = 0.4 · 5000 · (1 − 1/2) = 0.4 · 5000 · 1/2 = 1000 per unit time.
questions.explanation: Maximum of the parabolic function P(1 − P/K) in P is at P = K/2; substitute to get the max rate rK/4.

units.label: Unit 6: Integration and Accumulation of Change

question_types.label: Compute a left, right, or midpoint Riemann sum for a given partition
question_types.notes: Given n subintervals of [a,b], evaluate f at sample points (left/right/midpoint) and form Σf(x_i)·Δx.

questions.label: Q32-1

ordered_content.value: Let L_n denote the left-endpoint Riemann sum using n subintervals. Compute L_4 for f(x) = 1/(x - 1) on [2, 3].

questions.explanation: Identify Δx = (b−a)/n, list left endpoints, evaluate f, multiply by Δx.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q32-2

ordered_content.value: Compute R_4 (right-endpoint Riemann sum with 4 subintervals) for g(x) = cos(πx) on [0, 1].

questions.explanation: Right-endpoint version uses x_1, x_2, ..., x_n as sample points.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q32-3

ordered_content.value: Compute the left and right Riemann sums, L_4 and R_4, respectively, for f(x) = 2 − |x| on [−2, 2] and find their average.

questions.answer: Δx = 4/4 = 1. Partition: −2, −1, 0, 1, 2. f values: 0, 1, 2, 1, 0. L_4 uses f(−2), f(−1), f(0), f(1) = 0, 1, 2, 1; L_4 = (0+1+2+1)(1) = 4. R_4 uses f(−1), f(0), f(1), f(2) = 1, 2, 1, 0; R_4 = 4. Average = 4. Exact area (triangle with base 4, height 2) = 4, so the estimate matches exactly.
questions.explanation: Compute both endpoint sums; average often improves accuracy (and equals exact for piecewise-linear on matched partitions).

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

question_types.label: Express the limit of a Riemann sum as a definite integral
question_types.notes: Recognize lim_{n→∞} Σf(x_i*)Δx = ∫_a^b f(x) dx; extract the function and interval from the sum.

questions.label: Q33-1

ordered_content.value: Express the limit lim_{n→∞} Σ_{i=1}^n (x_i*) Δx, where the x_i* lie in [1, 3] and Δx is the width of a regular partition, as a definite integral.

questions.answer: The sum has the form Σ f(x_i*)Δx with f(x) = x on [1, 3]. So the limit equals ∫_1^3 x dx = [x²/2]_1^3 = 9/2 − 1/2 = 4.
questions.explanation: Read off the integrand from what's being evaluated at x_i* and the interval from where the x_i* live.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q33-2

ordered_content.value: Given L_n = (1/n) Σ_{i=1}^n (i−1)/n, express its limit as n→∞ as a definite integral.

questions.answer: Here Δx = 1/n, so [a,b] has length 1. Sample points (i−1)/n are left endpoints of a partition of [0, 1]. Integrand is the identity function. Limit = ∫_0^1 x dx = 1/2.
questions.explanation: Recognize Δx = 1/n ⇒ interval length 1; sample points (i−1)/n range over [0, 1].

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q33-3

ordered_content.value: Given R_n = (3/n) Σ_{i=1}^n (3 + 3i/n), express its limit as n→∞ as a definite integral, then evaluate it.

questions.answer: Δx = 3/n, so interval has length 3. Sample points 3 + 3i/n are right endpoints of a partition of [3, 6]. Integrand is the identity. Limit = ∫_3^6 x dx = [x²/2]_3^6 = 18 − 9/2 = 27/2.
questions.explanation: Match Δx to interval width; the constant shift in sample points tells you the left endpoint a.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

question_types.label: Evaluate a definite integral using area formulas and properties of integrals
question_types.notes: When the integrand is piecewise linear, a circle segment, or |ax+b|, compute ∫ by summing signed areas; combine via linearity of the integral.

questions.label: Q34-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_0^3 (3 − x) dx using area formulas.

questions.answer: The region under y = 3 − x from x = 0 to x = 3 is a right triangle with legs 3 and 3. Area = (1/2)(3)(3) = 9/2.
questions.explanation: Sketch the region; use the triangle area formula directly.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q34-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_{-2}^2 √(4 − x²) dx using area formulas.

questions.answer: The integrand is the upper half of x² + y² = 4 (a circle of radius 2). ∫ is the area of a semicircle of radius 2: (1/2) π (2)² = 2π.
questions.explanation: Recognize y = √(r² − x²) as the upper semicircle of radius r.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q34-3

ordered_content.value: Suppose ∫_0^4 f(x) dx = 5 and ∫_0^2 f(x) dx = −3, and ∫_0^4 g(x) dx = −1 and ∫_0^2 g(x) dx = 2. Compute ∫_2^4 (f(x) − g(x)) dx.

questions.explanation: Use additivity ∫_0^4 = ∫_0^2 + ∫_2^4 to back out ∫_2^4, then apply linearity.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

question_types.label: Differentiate an accumulation function using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1
question_types.notes: If F(x) = ∫_a^{g(x)} f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(g(x)) · g'(x). Handle chain-rule cases and symmetric limits.

questions.label: Q35-1

ordered_content.value: Find d/dx [∫_1^x e^{-t²} dt].

questions.answer: By FTC Part 1, the derivative of an accumulation function with upper limit x is the integrand evaluated at x: d/dx = e^{-x²}.
questions.explanation: FTC Part 1 in its simplest form: derivative of ∫_a^x f(t) dt is f(x).

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q35-2

ordered_content.value: Find d/dx [∫_x^{2x} t dt].

questions.answer: Split: ∫_x^{2x} t dt = ∫_0^{2x} t dt − ∫_0^x t dt. Differentiating with FTC + chain rule: d/dx = (2x)·2 − x = 4x − x = 3x. (Alternative check: ∫_x^{2x} t dt = [t²/2]_x^{2x} = 2x² − x²/2 = 3x²/2; derivative = 3x.)
questions.explanation: When both limits depend on x, split the integral or apply Leibniz: d/dx ∫_{a(x)}^{b(x)} f(t) dt = f(b(x))b'(x) − f(a(x))a'(x).

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q35-3

ordered_content.value: Find d/dx [∫_0^{sin x} √(1 − t²) dt].

questions.answer: By FTC + chain rule: d/dx = √(1 − sin²x) · cos x = √(cos²x) · cos x = |cos x| · cos x. On intervals where cos x ≥ 0, this simplifies to cos²x.
questions.explanation: Upper limit is a function of x, so multiply by its derivative.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

question_types.label: Evaluate a definite integral analytically using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
question_types.notes: Find an antiderivative F, then compute F(b) − F(a). Uses basic antiderivative rules for powers, trig, exp.

questions.label: Q36-1

ordered_content.value: Compute ∫ (√x − 1/√x) dx.

questions.explanation: Power rule: ∫ x^n dx = x^{n+1}/(n+1) for n ≠ −1.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q36-2

ordered_content.value: Compute ∫_0^π (sin x − cos x) dx.

questions.answer: Antiderivative: −cos x − sin x. Evaluate: [−cos x − sin x]_0^π = (−cos π − sin π) − (−cos 0 − sin 0) = (1 − 0) − (−1 − 0) = 1 + 1 = 2.
questions.explanation: Antiderivative of sin is −cos; of cos is sin. Subtract the evaluations at the two limits.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q36-3

ordered_content.value: Compute ∫(e^{2x} − (1/2) e^{x/2}) dx.

questions.explanation: ∫ e^{kx} dx = (1/k) e^{kx}.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

question_types.label: Apply the Net Change Theorem to compute displacement and total distance
question_types.notes: Displacement = ∫_a^b v(t) dt; total distance = ∫_a^b |v(t)| dt. Split at zeros of v when signs change.

questions.label: Q37-1

ordered_content.value: A particle moves along a straight line with velocity v(t) = 4 − 2t for 0 ≤ t ≤ 2 (meters per second). Find the displacement and the total distance traveled up to t = 2.

questions.answer: Displacement = ∫_0^2 (4 − 2t) dt = [4t − t²]_0^2 = 8 − 4 = 4 m. For total distance, check sign of v: v ≥ 0 on [0, 2] (v(2) = 0 but v > 0 on (0, 2)). So total distance = |4| = 4 m (same as displacement here).
questions.explanation: Displacement = ∫v; distance = ∫|v|. When v doesn't change sign, they coincide in magnitude.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q37-2

ordered_content.value: A particle moves along a line with velocity v(t) = t² − 3t − 18 for 0 ≤ t ≤ 6. Find the displacement and the total distance traveled.

questions.answer: Displacement = ∫_0^6 (t² − 3t − 18) dt = [t³/3 − 3t²/2 − 18t]_0^6 = 72 − 54 − 108 = −90 m (i.e., 90 m backward net). For distance, factor v = (t−6)(t+3); v = 0 at t = 6 (and t = −3 outside interval). v < 0 on [0, 6). So total distance = |−90| = 90 m.
questions.explanation: Factor v to find sign changes on the interval; integrate |v| piecewise if signs do change.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q37-3

ordered_content.value: A particle moves with velocity v(t) = t² − 4 on [0, 3]. Find the displacement and the total distance traveled.

questions.explanation: Find where v changes sign; split the interval and take absolute values piecewise for total distance.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

question_types.label: Evaluate an indefinite integral using u-substitution
question_types.notes: Identify u as an inner function so that du appears (up to a constant) elsewhere in the integrand; rewrite, integrate in u, back-substitute.

questions.label: Q38-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ (x + 1)^4 dx using the substitution u = x + 1.

questions.explanation: A linear u-substitution handles any (ax+b)^n integrand.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q38-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ x/√(x² + 1) dx using the substitution u = x² + 1.

questions.explanation: x dx matches half of du; factor out the 1/2.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q38-3

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ cos³ θ dθ using the identity cos²θ = 1 − sin²θ and a substitution u = sin θ.

questions.answer: Rewrite: cos³θ = cos²θ · cos θ = (1 − sin²θ) cos θ. u = sin θ, du = cos θ dθ. Integral becomes ∫(1 − u²) du = u − u³/3 + C = sin θ − sin³θ/3 + C.
questions.explanation: For odd powers of sine/cosine, save one factor to form du and convert the rest using the Pythagorean identity.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

question_types.label: Evaluate a definite integral using u-substitution with transformed limits
question_types.notes: Substitute u for an inner function, transform the limits a→u(a), b→u(b), and evaluate entirely in u without back-substituting.

questions.label: Q39-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_0^1 x(1 − x)^{99} dx via u-substitution with u = 1 − x.

questions.explanation: Transform the limits, absorb the sign from −du by flipping limits.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q39-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_0^{π/2} sin x · cos x dx via the substitution u = sin x.

questions.answer: u = sin x, du = cos x dx. Limits: x = 0 → u = 0; x = π/2 → u = 1. ∫_0^1 u du = [u²/2]_0^1 = 1/2.
questions.explanation: Limit transformation makes this a one-step integral.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q39-3

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_1^2 (x − 1)^5 dx via u-substitution.

questions.explanation: Linear substitutions shift the interval; evaluate directly in u.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

question_types.label: Evaluate an integral that produces an exponential or logarithm
question_types.notes: Recognize ∫ e^u du = e^u + C, ∫ du/u = ln|u| + C. Identify u so du appears (up to a constant).

questions.label: Q40-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ (ln x)/x dx.

questions.answer: u = ln x, du = dx/x. ∫ u du = u²/2 + C = (ln x)²/2 + C.
questions.explanation: Classic substitution for log-times-reciprocal integrands.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q40-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ x e^{-x²} dx.

questions.answer: u = −x², du = −2x dx ⇒ x dx = −du/2. ∫(−1/2) e^u du = −e^u/2 + C = −e^{-x²}/2 + C.
questions.explanation: Match the polynomial factor against du from the exponent's substitution.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q40-3

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ tan θ dθ.

questions.answer: tan θ = sin θ / cos θ. u = cos θ, du = −sin θ dθ. ∫−du/u = −ln|u| + C = −ln|cos θ| + C (equivalently ln|sec θ| + C).
questions.explanation: Rewrite as sin/cos; substitute u = cos θ; get a log.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

question_types.label: Evaluate an integral that produces an inverse trigonometric function
question_types.notes: ∫ du/√(1−u²) = arcsin u + C; ∫ du/(1+u²) = arctan u + C. Use substitution or complete the square as needed.

questions.label: Q41-1

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫_0^{√3/2} dx/√(1 − x²).

questions.answer: Antiderivative: arcsin x. [arcsin x]_0^{√3/2} = arcsin(√3/2) − arcsin(0) = π/3 − 0 = π/3.
questions.explanation: Direct formula ∫ dx/√(1−x²) = arcsin x.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q41-2

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ dx/(9 + x²).

questions.explanation: Pull out the constant in the denominator; ∫ dx/(a² + x²) = (1/a) arctan(x/a) + C.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

questions.label: Q41-3

ordered_content.value: Evaluate ∫ dx / (|x|·√(x² − 1)) for x > 1.

questions.answer: Direct formula: ∫ dx/(|x| √(x² − 1)) = arcsec|x| + C = arcsec x + C (for x > 1).
questions.explanation: Recognize the arcsec antiderivative form.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 1 Integration (§1.1–1.7)

subject: ap_calculus_bc
source_label: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)
custom_notes: Sections 7.1 Parametric Equations, 7.2 Calculus of Parametric Curves, 7.3 Polar Coordinates, 7.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates. Surface-area-of-revolution problems for parametric/polar curves are outside AP Calculus BC scope and were excluded. Source: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 (Strang, Herman), CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.
