units.label: Unit 9: Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions

question_types.label: Eliminate the parameter to express a parametric curve in Cartesian form
question_types.notes: Solve one equation for t (or use a Pythagorean identity), substitute into the other, and identify the resulting curve.

questions.label: Q42-1

ordered_content.value: Convert the parametric equations x = t² − 1, y = t/2 (with t ≥ −1) into rectangular form by eliminating the parameter. No sketch is necessary.

questions.answer: From y = t/2, t = 2y. Substitute: x = (2y)² − 1 = 4y² − 1. Cartesian equation: x = 4y² − 1 (a rightward-opening parabola with vertex (−1, 0)).
questions.explanation: Solve the simpler equation for t, then substitute.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q42-2

ordered_content.value: Convert the parametric equations x = 3 cos t, y = 4 sin t into rectangular form by eliminating the parameter.

questions.answer: From the equations, cos t = x/3 and sin t = y/4. Pythagorean identity: (x/3)² + (y/4)² = 1, i.e., x²/9 + y²/16 = 1 — an ellipse.
questions.explanation: When x and y are each expressed via sin t or cos t with different amplitudes, square and add to eliminate t via sin² + cos² = 1.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q42-3

ordered_content.value: Convert the parametric equations x = 2t + 1, y = t² − 3 into rectangular form by eliminating the parameter.

questions.answer: From x = 2t + 1, t = (x − 1)/2. Substitute: y = ((x − 1)/2)² − 3 = (x − 1)²/4 − 3. Cartesian: y = (x − 1)²/4 − 3, a parabola with vertex (1, −3).
questions.explanation: Linear in x makes solving for t straightforward; substitute into the quadratic in y.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

question_types.label: Find dy/dx for a parametric curve and write the tangent line at a given t
question_types.notes: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt); evaluate at the given parameter to get the slope, then use point-slope with (x(t₀), y(t₀)).

questions.label: Q43-1

ordered_content.value: For the parametric curve x = 3 sin t, y = 3 cos t, find the slope of the tangent line and the equation of the tangent line at t = π/4.

questions.answer: dx/dt = 3 cos t, dy/dt = −3 sin t. dy/dx = (−3 sin t)/(3 cos t) = −tan t. At t = π/4: slope = −1. Point: x = 3 sin(π/4) = 3√2/2, y = 3 cos(π/4) = 3√2/2. Tangent: y − 3√2/2 = −1·(x − 3√2/2), i.e., y = −x + 3√2.
questions.explanation: Compute dx/dt and dy/dt separately; dy/dx is their quotient.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q43-2

ordered_content.value: For the parametric curve x = 2t, y = t³ at t = −1, find the slope of the tangent line and the equation of the tangent line.

questions.answer: dx/dt = 2, dy/dt = 3t². dy/dx = 3t²/2. At t = −1: slope = 3/2. Point: x = −2, y = −1. Tangent: y + 1 = (3/2)(x + 2), i.e., y = (3/2)x + 2.
questions.explanation: Plug t = −1 into both dy/dx and the point formulas (x(−1), y(−1)).

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q43-3

ordered_content.value: For the parametric curve x = √t, y = 2t at t = 4, find the slope of the tangent line and the equation of the tangent line.

questions.answer: dx/dt = 1/(2√t), dy/dt = 2. dy/dx = 2 / (1/(2√t)) = 4√t. At t = 4: slope = 4·2 = 8. Point: x = 2, y = 8. Tangent: y − 8 = 8(x − 2), i.e., y = 8x − 8.
questions.explanation: Clean up the quotient of dy/dt and dx/dt before evaluating.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

question_types.label: Find the second derivative d²y/dx² for a parametric curve
question_types.notes: d²y/dx² = d/dx (dy/dx) = [d/dt(dy/dx)] / (dx/dt). Differentiate the slope with respect to t first, then divide by dx/dt.

questions.label: Q44-1

ordered_content.value: Find d²y/dx² for the parametric curve x = t⁴ − 1, y = t − t².

questions.explanation: Quotient rule on dy/dx to get d/dt(dy/dx), then divide by dx/dt.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q44-2

ordered_content.value: Find d²y/dx² for the parametric curve x = sin(πt), y = cos(πt).

questions.answer: dx/dt = π cos(πt), dy/dt = −π sin(πt). dy/dx = −sin(πt)/cos(πt) = −tan(πt). d/dt[−tan(πt)] = −π sec²(πt). Divide by dx/dt = π cos(πt): d²y/dx² = −sec²(πt)/cos(πt) = −sec³(πt)/1 = −1/cos³(πt).
questions.explanation: Simplify dy/dx to a familiar form first; differentiate that; divide by dx/dt.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q44-3

ordered_content.value: Find d²y/dx² for the parametric curve x = e^{-t}, y = t·e^{2t}.

questions.explanation: Product and chain rules for dy/dt; then the standard parametric second-derivative formula.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

question_types.label: Compute the arc length of a curve given parametrically
question_types.notes: L = ∫_α^β √((dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²) dt. Simplify the integrand when possible; otherwise set up and numerically evaluate.

questions.label: Q45-1

ordered_content.value: Find the arc length of the curve x = 4t + 3, y = 3t − 2 on the interval 0 ≤ t ≤ 2.

questions.explanation: For a straight-line parametrization, the integrand is constant = magnitude of the direction vector times the parameter span.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q45-2

ordered_content.value: Find the arc length of the curve x = cos(2t), y = sin(2t) on the interval 0 ≤ t ≤ π/2.

questions.answer: dx/dt = −2 sin(2t), dy/dt = 2 cos(2t). (dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)² = 4 sin²(2t) + 4 cos²(2t) = 4. √(·) = 2. L = ∫_0^{π/2} 2 dt = π.
questions.explanation: Parametrizes the unit circle at double speed; arc length equals speed × time.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q45-3

ordered_content.value: Find the arc length of the curve x = (1/3)t³, y = (1/2)t² on the interval 0 ≤ t ≤ 1.

questions.explanation: Factor under the radical; u-substitution handles the resulting integrand.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

question_types.label: Analyze planar motion: velocity vector, speed, and position via definite integrals
question_types.notes: For a particle with position (x(t), y(t)): velocity v(t) = (x'(t), y'(t)); speed = |v(t)| = √(x'² + y'²); distance = ∫|v| dt; displacement vector = ∫v dt.

questions.label: Q46-1

ordered_content.value: A particle moves in the plane with position x(t) = t² − 2t, y(t) = t³ − 3t for 0 ≤ t ≤ 2. Find the velocity vector and the speed at t = 1.

questions.answer: x'(t) = 2t − 2, y'(t) = 3t² − 3. Velocity vector at t = 1: (2(1) − 2, 3 − 3) = (0, 0). Speed = √(0² + 0²) = 0. (The particle is momentarily at rest at t = 1.)
questions.explanation: Velocity is the vector (x'(t), y'(t)); speed is its magnitude.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q46-2

ordered_content.value: A particle has velocity vector v(t) = (2t, 3). If its position at t = 0 is (1, 0), find its position at t = 2.

questions.explanation: Integrate velocity componentwise and add to initial position.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q46-3

ordered_content.value: A particle moves along a curve with position (x(t), y(t)) = (cos t, sin t) on 0 ≤ t ≤ π. Find the total distance traveled.

questions.answer: Velocity: (−sin t, cos t). Speed: √(sin²t + cos²t) = 1. Distance = ∫_0^π 1 dt = π.
questions.explanation: Total distance = ∫ speed dt. For uniform speed 1 on [0, π], distance = π (half the unit circle's circumference).

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

question_types.label: Convert between polar and rectangular coordinates and equations
question_types.notes: Use x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ, r² = x² + y², tan θ = y/x to convert points and equations between representations.

questions.label: Q47-1

ordered_content.value: Find rectangular coordinates for the point (2, 5π/4) given in polar coordinates.

questions.explanation: Apply x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ directly.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q47-2

ordered_content.value: Convert the rectangular equation x² + y² = 16 to polar form.

questions.answer: x² + y² = r², so r² = 16, giving r = 4 (taking positive root).
questions.explanation: The identity r² = x² + y² makes circles centered at the origin trivial in polar form.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q47-3

ordered_content.value: Convert the rectangular equation 3x − y = 2 to polar form.

questions.answer: Substitute x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ: 3r cos θ − r sin θ = 2, so r(3 cos θ − sin θ) = 2, giving r = 2/(3 cos θ − sin θ).
questions.explanation: Factor r out after substituting, then solve for r.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

question_types.label: Compute the area enclosed by a polar curve using a definite integral
question_types.notes: Area = (1/2) ∫_α^β [r(θ)]² dθ, with θ ranging over the interval that traces the bounded region.

questions.label: Q48-1

ordered_content.value: Determine a definite integral that represents the area enclosed by the polar curve r = 4 (a circle of radius 4), and evaluate it.

questions.explanation: Constant-r polar curve gives a circle; the integral reproduces πr².

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q48-2

ordered_content.value: Compute the area enclosed by one petal of the four-petal rose r = 4 cos(2θ).

questions.answer: One petal traces out as 2θ goes from −π/2 to π/2, i.e., θ from −π/4 to π/4. A = (1/2) ∫_{-π/4}^{π/4} 16 cos²(2θ) dθ = 8 ∫_{-π/4}^{π/4} cos²(2θ) dθ. Use cos²u = (1 + cos 2u)/2: = 8 ∫_{-π/4}^{π/4} (1 + cos 4θ)/2 dθ = 4 [θ + sin(4θ)/4]_{-π/4}^{π/4} = 4·[(π/4 + 0) − (−π/4 + 0)] = 4·(π/2) = 2π.
questions.explanation: Find the θ-range for one petal (r = 0 on each side); apply the area formula with the power-reduction identity.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q48-3

ordered_content.value: Compute the area of the region enclosed by the cardioid r = 1 + sin θ.

questions.answer: Full curve traced as θ from 0 to 2π. A = (1/2) ∫_0^{2π} (1 + sin θ)² dθ = (1/2) ∫_0^{2π} (1 + 2 sin θ + sin²θ) dθ. Term-by-term: ∫2π 1 dθ = 2π; ∫_0^{2π} 2 sin θ dθ = 0; ∫_0^{2π} sin²θ dθ = π. Total: (1/2)(2π + 0 + π) = 3π/2.
questions.explanation: Expand (1 + sin θ)² and integrate term-by-term over a full period.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

question_types.label: Compute the area between two polar curves or the inner loop of a limaçon
question_types.notes: For region between r = f(θ) (outer) and r = g(θ) (inner): A = (1/2) ∫ (f² − g²) dθ over the angular interval. For inner loops: find θ where r = 0 and integrate over that interval.

questions.label: Q49-1

ordered_content.value: Find the area of the inner loop of the limaçon r = 2 − 3 sin θ.

questions.answer: r = 0 when sin θ = 2/3, i.e., θ = arcsin(2/3) and θ = π − arcsin(2/3). Let α = arcsin(2/3). The inner loop is traced where r ≤ 0, which occurs between these θ values. A = (1/2) ∫_α^{π−α} (2 − 3 sin θ)² dθ. Expand: (2 − 3 sin θ)² = 4 − 12 sin θ + 9 sin²θ = 4 − 12 sin θ + (9/2)(1 − cos 2θ). Integrate term-by-term over [α, π − α]; the integral evaluates to a closed-form expression in terms of α = arcsin(2/3): A = (1/2)[(4 + 9/2)(π − 2α) − 12·2 cos α + (9/4)(−sin 2(π−α) + sin 2α)]. Numerically α ≈ 0.7297, yielding A ≈ 0.544.
questions.explanation: Find where r = 0 to get the θ-interval for the inner loop; apply the polar-area formula there.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q49-2

ordered_content.value: Find the area common to the circles r = 2 and r = 4 cos θ.

questions.answer: r = 4 cos θ is a circle of radius 2 centered at (2, 0). r = 2 is a circle of radius 2 at origin. They intersect where 4 cos θ = 2, i.e., cos θ = 1/2, θ = ±π/3. Common region: between θ = −π/3 and π/3 use r = 2 as inner bound (actually here r = 2 is the smaller r — check sample values); for the sector not covered by r = 2, use r = 4 cos θ. Symmetry allows: A = 2·[(1/2) ∫_0^{π/3} 2² dθ + (1/2) ∫_{π/3}^{π/2} (4 cos θ)² dθ] = ∫_0^{π/3} 4 dθ + ∫_{π/3}^{π/2} 16 cos²θ dθ = 4π/3 + 8∫_{π/3}^{π/2} (1 + cos 2θ) dθ = 4π/3 + 8[θ + sin(2θ)/2]_{π/3}^{π/2} = 4π/3 + 8[(π/2 + 0) − (π/3 + (√3)/4)] = 4π/3 + 8(π/6 − √3/4) = 4π/3 + 4π/3 − 2√3 = 8π/3 − 2√3.
questions.explanation: Find intersection angles, split the region using the smaller r in each sub-interval, and use symmetry.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

questions.label: Q49-3

ordered_content.value: Set up a definite integral for the area of the region inside the cardioid r = 1 + cos θ and outside the circle r = 1.

questions.explanation: Find where the two curves meet, then compute (1/2) ∫ (outer² − inner²) dθ.

source_reference.worksheet_name: OpenStax Calculus Volume 2 — Ch. 7 Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates (§7.1–7.4)

subject: ap_calculus_bc
source_label: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set

question_types.label: P-Series Convergence Analysis
question_types.notes: Focused on identifying convergence/divergence of p-series based on exponent values.

questions.label: Q1

ordered_content.value:
Which of the following series converge?
I. $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{0.99}}$
II. $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2+1}$
III. $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n \ln n}$

questions.answer: I is a p-series with p=0.99 <= 1 (diverges). II converges by comparison with 1/n^2. III diverges by integral test.
questions.explanation: Apply p-series test for I, comparison for II, and integral test for III.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set

questions.label: Q2

ordered_content.value: The series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^p}$ converges if and only if

questions.answer: By definition of p-series convergence.
questions.explanation: Standard theorem for p-series.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set

questions.label: Q3

ordered_content.value: Which of the following series diverge?

questions.answer: n^(2/3) has p=2/3, which is <= 1, so it diverges.
questions.explanation: Identify p in n^(2/3) and apply p-series rule.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set

question_types.label: Integral Test Application
question_types.notes: Requires conversion of series to improper integrals for convergence testing.

questions.label: Q4

ordered_content.value: Use the Integral Test to determine the convergence or divergence of the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{n}{n^2+1}$.

questions.answer: Evaluate integral of x/(x^2+1) from 1 to infinity. Result is limit as b->inf of 0.5*ln(b^2+1) - 0.5*ln(2), which is infinity. Diverges.
questions.explanation: Convert to integral, use u-substitution, and find the limit.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set

questions.label: Q5

ordered_content.value: Determine whether the series $\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n(\ln n)^2}$ converges or diverges. Justify your answer using the Integral Test.

questions.answer: Integral from 2 to infinity of 1/(x(ln x)^2) dx. Let u=ln x, then integral is 1/u^2 du. Evaluates to 1/ln 2. Converges.
questions.explanation: Apply integral test with u-substitution for the log term.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set

questions.label: Q6

ordered_content.value: Check the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2+1}$ using the integral test.

questions.answer: Integral of 1/(x^2+1) is arctan(x). Limit as x->inf is pi/2. Converges.
questions.explanation: Standard integral for arctan.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set

question_types.label: Comparison Test Strategy
question_types.notes: Focuses on selecting a 'benchmark' series (p-series or geometric) for DCT or LCT.

questions.label: Q7

ordered_content.value: Which of the following statements about the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^n - 1}$ is true?

options: (A) The series diverges by the nth term test.
(B) The series converges by comparison with $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^n}$.
(C) The series diverges by comparison with $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}$.
(D) The series converges by the p-series test.
questions.correct_option: (B) The series converges by comparison with $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^n}$.
questions.answer: LCT with geometric series 1/2^n gives limit of 1. Since 1/2^n converges, the original series converges.
questions.explanation: Use LCT for series involving exponential terms.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set

questions.label: Q8

ordered_content.value: The series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{n+1}{n^2+2n+3}$ behaves most like which of the following series when using the Limit Comparison Test?

questions.answer: Comparing dominant terms n/n^2 simplifies to 1/n.
questions.explanation: Identify the highest degree terms in the numerator and denominator.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set

questions.label: Q9

ordered_content.value: Use the Direct Comparison Test to determine if $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{3^n + n}$ converges or diverges.

questions.answer: 1/(3^n + n) < 1/3^n for all n >= 1. Since sum of 1/3^n is a convergent geometric series, the original series converges by DCT.
questions.explanation: Establish the inequality and cite the comparison series convergence.

source_reference.worksheet_name: AP Calc BC — Unit 10 Series Practice Set
